name: Tsukuyomi
title: Japanese - Tsukuyomi
symbols: Rabbit (usagi - associated with the moon in East Asian tradition)
the fifteenth night of the eighth month (harvest moon)

origins.title: Origins
origins.content: Tsukuyomi was born from the primordial creator god Izanagi during his purification ritual after escaping from Yomi, the underworld. As Izanagi washed his face in the Tachibana River at Awagihara in Hyuga province, three noble kami emerged: Amaterasu from his left eye, Tsukuyomi from his right eye, and Susanoo from his nose. These three 'noble children' (mihashira no uzu no miko) were assigned to rule the celestial domains. Izanagi entrusted Tsukuyomi with the realm of night, making him the sovereign of darkness and the moon's cycles.

mythology.title: Mythology
mythology.content: The most significant myth involving Tsukuyomi explains the eternal separation of day and night. Amaterasu sent Tsukuyomi as her representative to visit Uke Mochi, the goddess of food. When Uke Mochi produced a feast by spitting fish from her mouth, rice from her nose, and various foods from other orifices, Tsukuyomi was so disgusted by this manner of producing food that he killed her in rage. When Amaterasu learned of this violence, she declared she would never again look upon her brother's face, and moved to a separate part of the sky. This divine separation is why the sun and moon never appear together, eternally chasing but never meeting in the heavens.

attributes.title: Attributes
attributes.content: Tsukuyomi embodies the mysterious, calculating nature of the moon itself. He governs the passage of time through lunar cycles, and his name literally means 'moon-reading' or 'moon-counting,' reflecting his role in the ancient Japanese lunar calendar. Unlike his boisterous brother Susanoo or his radiant sister Amaterasu, Tsukuyomi is characterized by restraint, order, and a strict sense of propriety - qualities that ironically led to his violent act against Uke Mochi. He rules over nocturnal activities including hunting, divination, and the mysterious transitions between day and night.

culturalRole.title: Cultural Role
culturalRole.content: Despite his relative obscurity compared to Amaterasu, Tsukuyomi holds deep cultural significance in Japan. The Tsukimi (moon-viewing) festival, traditionally held on the fifteenth night of the eighth lunar month, honors the harvest moon with offerings of dango and susuki grass. The moon rabbit (tsuki no usagi), a figure seen in the moon's surface, connects to East Asian folklore where a rabbit pounds mochi on the moon. Ancient Japanese time-keeping relied on lunar cycles, making Tsukuyomi's domain essential for agricultural planning, religious festivals, and the rhythms of daily life. His influence persists in Japanese aesthetics, where the moon represents wabi-sabi beauty, impermanence, and the poetic appreciation of nature's subtle transformations.

sources.title: Kojiki
sources.description: Japan's oldest chronicle, containing the birth narrative of Tsukuyomi

sources.title: Nihon Shoki
sources.description: Official chronicle with variant accounts of the moon god's mythology

sources.title: Shinto: The Way Home
sources.author: Thomas Kasulis
sources.description: Scholarly analysis of Shinto cosmology and kami relationships

description: Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto is the Japanese Shinto god of the moon, night, and the passage of time. Born from the right eye of Izanagi during his purification ritual, Tsukuyomi is one of the three noble children alongside his sister Amaterasu (sun) and brother Susanoo (storms). He rules over the night realm and governs the lunar calendar that ordered ancient Japanese life. His killing of the food goddess Uke Mochi caused his eternal separation from Amaterasu, explaining why the sun and moon never share the sky. Though less prominently worshipped than his siblings, Tsukuyomi's influence pervades Japanese culture through moon-viewing traditions, the moon rabbit legend, and the aesthetic appreciation of nocturnal beauty.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: God of Wisdom & Writing | Egyptian Mythology

worshipPractices.name: Offerings to the Moon
worshipPractices.description: While not as prevalent as sun worship, offerings of rice and sake are sometimes made to the moon, particularly during times of transition or uncertainty. These offerings are intended to appease Tsukuyomi and ensure safe passage through the night.

worshipPractices.name: Silent Contemplation
worshipPractices.description: Given Tsukuyomi's association with the night and introspection, quiet contemplation under the moonlight is seen as a way to connect with the deity. This practice involves reflecting on one's life and seeking guidance in the stillness of the night.

festivals.name: Tsukimi (Moon Viewing)
festivals.description: A harvest festival dedicated to the moon, although not exclusively to Tsukuyomi. People offer seasonal fruits, vegetables, and especially Tsukimi dango (rice dumplings) to the moon. Viewing the full moon is the central activity, often accompanied by poetry and music.

cultural.modernLegacy: Tsukuyomi continues to appear in modern Japanese popular culture, particularly in manga, anime, and video games. Often depicted as a mysterious and powerful figure, Tsukuyomi embodies the enigmatic allure of the night and the moon, serving as a source of inspiration for artists and writers.

associations.name: Moon
associations.significance: Tsukuyomi is the god of the moon, directly embodying its characteristics and phases.

associations.name: Silver
associations.significance: Silver is often associated with the moon's light, reflecting Tsukuyomi's nocturnal domain.

associations.name: Two
associations.significance: Represents duality and balance, contrasting with Amaterasu as a sibling deity and representing night versus day.

associations.name: Time
associations.significance: Tsukuyomi governs the passage of time, particularly as measured by lunar cycles.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: Water is often linked to the moon and the tides, reflecting Tsukuyomi's connection to the night and its mysteries.

associations.name: Hare
associations.significance: The Moon Rabbit is a common folklore figure associated with the moon in East Asian cultures, linking it to Tsukuyomi's realm.

associations.name: Reflection
associations.significance: The moon reflects the sun's light, symbolizing Tsukuyomi's role as a reflector of Amaterasu's brilliance, albeit in a different context.

companions.name: Amaterasu
companions.description: Amaterasu is Tsukuyomi's sister, born from Izanagi's left eye. Although they are siblings, their relationship is strained after the Uke Mochi incident, leading to the separation of day and night.

companions.name: Susanoo
companions.description: Susanoo is Tsukuyomi's brother, born from Izanagi's nose. Their relationship is complex, marked by both familial ties and individual ambitions.

companions.name: Uke Mochi
companions.description: Uke Mochi is a food goddess that Tsukuyomi visited. Tsukuyomi's violent reaction to Uke Mochi's method of food production led to her death and a rift between him and Amaterasu.

companions.name: Izanagi
companions.description: Izanagi is Tsukuyomi's father, who created him during a purification ritual. As his parent, Izanagi initially held authority and care for Tsukuyomi.

extendedContent.title: Origins and Birth of Tsukuyomi
extendedContent.content:
Tsukuyomi's birth is a pivotal event in Japanese creation mythology, marking the establishment of the celestial order. According to the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, the primary sources for Shinto mythology, Tsukuyomi was born from the right eye of Izanagi-no-Mikoto as he purified himself in a river after his harrowing visit to Yomi, the land of the dead, to retrieve his deceased wife, Izanami. This act of purification was crucial in cleansing Izanagi of the defilement he encountered in the underworld. The birth of Tsukuyomi, alongside Amaterasu (born from the left eye) and Susanoo (born from the nose), signified the emergence of the three most important deities in the Shinto pantheon, each tasked with ruling over a specific domain of the cosmos.

Different versions of the myth exist, particularly in the Nihon Shoki, which presents alternative accounts of Tsukuyomi's birth. Some versions state that he was born from a white copper mirror held in Izanagi's right hand, while others suggest he was spontaneously generated. Despite these variations, the consistent theme is Tsukuyomi's divine origin and his inherent connection to Izanagi's purity and power. The act of being born from Izanagi's body establishes him as a fundamental aspect of the creator god's being and reinforces his status as a central figure in Shinto cosmology.

The order in which the three deities were born is also significant. Amaterasu, the sun goddess, is generally considered the eldest and most important, followed by Tsukuyomi and then Susanoo. This hierarchy reflects the relative importance of the sun, moon, and storms in ancient Japanese society. While Amaterasu illuminates and nurtures, Tsukuyomi governs the night and regulates time, and Susanoo, in his tumultuous nature, brings both destruction and renewal.

extendedContent.title: Tsukuyomi's Role in Cosmic Order
extendedContent.content:
Tsukuyomi's primary role is to govern the night and the passage of time, specifically through the lunar calendar. In ancient Japan, the lunar calendar was essential for agriculture, festivals, and other important societal activities. Tsukuyomi's influence on the moon directly impacted the scheduling of these events, making him a vital deity for maintaining order and predictability in daily life. While Amaterasu provides light and warmth during the day, Tsukuyomi offers a contrasting presence of serenity and reflection during the night. This duality is crucial for balancing the cosmos and ensuring harmony.

However, Tsukuyomi is often portrayed as a more passive figure compared to his siblings. He is less involved in direct interactions with the mortal realm and his myths are fewer and less detailed. This relative obscurity could be attributed to the societal emphasis on the sun and agriculture, with Amaterasu taking precedence. Nevertheless, Tsukuyomi's quiet influence is undeniable. He represents the unseen forces that govern the night and the subtle shifts of time that shape human existence.

The contrast between Tsukuyomi and his siblings, Amaterasu and Susanoo, highlights the complex dynamics within the Shinto pantheon. While Amaterasu is associated with order and benevolence and Susanoo with chaos and impulsiveness, Tsukuyomi occupies a middle ground, representing a calm and measured approach. He embodies the importance of balance and the need for both light and darkness, activity and rest, in maintaining the cosmic equilibrium. This equilibrium is constantly threatened by Susanoo's disruptive actions, and it is the combined efforts of Amaterasu and Tsukuyomi that restore balance and harmony to the world.

extendedContent.title: The Tale of Tsukuyomi and Ukemochi: A Fatal Encounter
extendedContent.content:
The most well-known myth involving Tsukuyomi centers around his encounter with Ukemochi-no-Kami, the goddess of food. According to the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, Amaterasu sent Tsukuyomi to visit Ukemochi. During his visit, Ukemochi demonstrated her ability to create food by vomiting forth rice, fish, and other delicacies. While this act was meant to be a generous offering, Tsukuyomi was disgusted by the method of food production, deeming it unclean and disrespectful.

Enraged by what he perceived as a vile act, Tsukuyomi drew his sword and killed Ukemochi. When Amaterasu learned of Tsukuyomi's actions, she was deeply offended and declared that she would never look upon him again. This event is the mythological explanation for why the sun and moon are never seen together in the sky. The separation between Amaterasu and Tsukuyomi symbolizes the division between day and night and highlights the consequences of Tsukuyomi's impetuous act.

The myth of Tsukuyomi and Ukemochi offers insights into ancient Japanese values and beliefs regarding purity, food, and social etiquette. Tsukuyomi's reaction reflects a strong emphasis on cleanliness and the proper handling of food. It also illustrates the importance of respecting the deities and their offerings. While Ukemochi's intentions were good, Tsukuyomi's perception of her actions led to a tragic outcome, underscoring the potential for misunderstanding and conflict even among the gods. This story serves as a cautionary tale about the importance of perspective and the potential consequences of rash judgment.

extendedContent.title: Worship and Temples Dedicated to Tsukuyomi
extendedContent.content:
Compared to Amaterasu and Susanoo, Tsukuyomi's worship is less prominent and widespread in modern Shinto practice. There are fewer dedicated shrines and festivals explicitly honoring him. This scarcity is likely due to the greater emphasis on the sun goddess and the storm god in agricultural and societal contexts. However, Tsukuyomi is still revered in various Shinto shrines, often as a secondary deity alongside other kami. Some notable shrines dedicated to Tsukuyomi include the Tsukuyomi Shrine in Ise Grand Shrine (Naiku), one of the most important Shinto shrines in Japan, signifying his recognition within the highest levels of Shinto practice.

While dedicated festivals are rare, Tsukuyomi is often honored during lunar festivals and harvest celebrations, as the moon plays a crucial role in agriculture and the timing of these events. Offerings made to Tsukuyomi typically include white objects, reflecting the color associated with the moon. Rice, sake, and other agricultural products are also commonly presented as offerings to express gratitude for his influence on the lunar cycle and its impact on the harvest.

Despite his relatively less prominent role in popular worship, Tsukuyomi remains an important figure in Shinto cosmology and is revered by those who seek a deeper connection with the natural world and the cycles of time. His presence is felt through the moon's gentle light and the rhythmic patterns of the lunar calendar, reminding us of the subtle but profound forces that shape our lives. The continued reverence for Tsukuyomi ensures that the night and the passage of time are recognized as essential aspects of the divine order.

extendedContent.title: Iconography and Representation of Tsukuyomi
extendedContent.content:
Tsukuyomi's iconography is not as standardized or widely recognized as that of Amaterasu or Susanoo. Unlike his siblings, he lacks a definitive visual representation that is consistently depicted across different shrines and artworks. However, certain symbolic elements are often associated with him, reflecting his role as the god of the moon and night. These symbols include the moon itself, often depicted as a crescent or full moon, and nocturnal animals such as rabbits and owls, which are linked to the moon's mystique and the darkness of night.

In artistic representations, Tsukuyomi is often portrayed as a male deity dressed in traditional Japanese clothing, typically wearing robes of white or silver to symbolize the moon's color. He may be depicted holding a sword or a staff, representing his authority over the night realm. In some depictions, he is shown riding a white stag or a moon rabbit, further emphasizing his connection to the lunar sphere. However, these depictions are not universally consistent, and the specific details may vary depending on the artist and the context.

Due to his more passive nature compared to his siblings, Tsukuyomi is often portrayed with a calm and serene expression, reflecting his association with tranquility and introspection. His presence is typically subtle and understated, mirroring the moon's gentle light that illuminates the night without overpowering the darkness. The relative lack of a strong visual identity may contribute to his less prominent role in popular culture, but it also allows for a more open and flexible interpretation of his character and attributes.
